summary: Deep analysis of the agentsh Go codebase, PowerShell internals, and .NET platform APIs reveals a clear architectural path for agentpowershell: a C#/.NET daemon that uses named pipes for IPC, hosts PowerShell via the SDK with a custom PSHost and constrained InitialSessionState, evaluates YAML-based policies through a compiled rule engine, and enforces security through platform-specific mechanisms (Job Objects + minifilter on Windows, Landlock + seccomp on Linux, Endpoint Security on macOS).
keyFindings: agentsh uses a daemon+shim pattern where /bin/sh is replaced with a shim binary that contacts the daemon for policy decisions via IPC before executing commands through the real shell

The policy engine supports 10+ rule types (file, network, command, registry, signal, DNS redirect, connect redirect, unix socket, MCP, package) with first-match-wins evaluation and compiled glob/regex patterns
The event system has 70+ event types across 14 categories with a pub/sub broker and composite fan-out store (SQLite, JSONL, webhook, OTEL) supporting tamper-detection via HMAC chains

PowerShell's PSHost is a 1:1 host-to-runspace abstraction that controls UI but NOT command interception -- actual command interception requires InitialSessionState constraints, binary module proxy cmdlets, or NativeCommandProcessor wrapping

PowerShell's NativeCommandProcessor uses System.Diagnostics.Process internally, meaning OS-level enforcement (Job Objects, seccomp) is the only reliable way to prevent process execution bypass via direct .NET API calls

The agentsh Windows minifilter driver communicates via FilterPort using a structured protocol with MSG_POLICY_CHECK_FILE and MSG_POLICY_CHECK_REGISTRY messages, supporting async decisions with configurable cache TTL
ConstrainedLanguage mode in PowerShell prevents Add-Type, direct .NET type instantiation, and COM objects -- essential for preventing sandbox escape
Named pipes (System.IO.Pipes) provide cross-platform IPC in .NET, mapping to Win32 named pipes on Windows and Unix domain sockets on Linux/macOS
architecturalInsights: The daemon should be a long-running .NET process hosting the policy engine, event store, and approval manager, with the shim being a lightweight PowerShell module or custom host that contacts the daemon per command

Policy evaluation must happen BEFORE process creation, not after -- this requires intercepting at the PowerShell command pipeline level (pre-execution) and at the OS level (Job Objects with CREATE_SUSPENDED for process creation control)
Session-scoped policy engines with variable expansion enable workspace-aware policies without per-workspace policy files
The composite event store pattern (fan-out to multiple backends) should be adopted directly -- SQLite for queries, JSONL for rotation-based logs, webhooks for external integration
Process context chains (ancestry-based policy) are critical for AI agent sandboxing -- detecting shell laundering requires tracking process trees
The approval flow needs multiple modes (TTY, API, TOTP, WebAuthn, dialog) to support different deployment scenarios from local dev to headless CI
ETW on Windows provides PowerShell script block logging, process creation, file operations, and network activity without any modifications to the target process

windows: Job Objects (kernel32.dll) -- process containment, resource limits

risks: PowerShell bypass via -NoProfile flag renders profile-based enforcement insufficient
Direct .NET API calls bypass PowerShell command pipeline entirely -- ConstrainedLanguage mode + OS enforcement required
Windows minifilter driver requires kernel-mode signing certificate for production
macOS Endpoint Security requires System Extension entitlement from Apple
seccomp user-notify not available in all Linux environments -- need fallback to ptrace
Per-command IPC to daemon adds latency; need caching of policy decisions
Race conditions in process tree tracking when PIDs are recycled
